IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an important, practical problem—efficient joint syntactic and semantic parsing—and demonstrates a clear empirical win over prior joint models on CoNLL 2008/2009 using a novel application of stack LSTMs and greedy linear-time inference. From an experimental-methodology standpoint the work is persuasive: it compares to strong baselines, reports hyperparameters and releases code, and uses standard shared-task datasets and pretrained embeddings. The approach is likely to change practitioner choices (favoring compact, end-to-end learned transition models) and opens meaningful follow-ups (extensions of stack-LSTM state representations, better handling of rare predicate senses), but it is not fully transformative since it still relies on transition-based encoding choices and has some practical limitations (encoding errors for a small fraction of sentences and sense fallback). | B(4): The paper addresses the important problem of joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing by proposing a practical, transition-based model that uses stack LSTMs to represent full parser state and runs in linear-time greedy inference; it empirically outperforms prior joint models on CoNLL 2008/2009. From a structured-semantics perspective this demonstrates that integrated, learned state representations can mitigate pipeline fragility and make joint parsing competitive in speed and accuracy, which is likely to influence subsequent work. The availability of code and clear hyperparameter details increases the chance it will be adopted and extended, though the reported encoding failures and predicate-sense fallback indicate limitations that temper its transformative potential.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an important, practical problem—efficient joint syntactic and semantic parsing—and demonstrates a clear empirical win over prior joint models on CoNLL 2008/2009 using a novel application of stack LSTMs and greedy linear-time inference. From an experimental-methodology standpoint the work is persuasive: it compares to strong baselines, reports hyperparameters and releases code, and uses standard shared-task datasets and pretrained embeddings. The approach is likely to change practitioner choices (favoring compact, end-to-end learned transition models) and opens meaningful follow-ups (extensions of stack-LSTM state representations, better handling of rare predicate senses), but it is not fully transformative since it still relies on transition-based encoding choices and has some practical limitations (encoding errors for a small fraction of sentences and sense fallback).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses the important problem of joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing by proposing a practical, transition-based model that uses stack LSTMs to represent full parser state and runs in linear-time greedy inference; it empirically outperforms prior joint models on CoNLL 2008/2009. From a structured-semantics perspective this demonstrates that integrated, learned state representations can mitigate pipeline fragility and make joint parsing competitive in speed and accuracy, which is likely to influence subsequent work. The availability of code and clear hyperparameter details increases the chance it will be adopted and extended, though the reported encoding failures and predicate-sense fallback indicate limitations that temper its transformative potential.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from introduction through method to experiments and conclusion, and its main contributions (a transition-based joint syntactic+semantic parser using stack LSTMs and linear-time greedy inference) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, with code release aiding reproducibility; figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective. Writing quality is good overall, though a couple of minor presentation issues remain (e.g., the 1.5% encoding failure and predicate-sense fallback are noted but could use slightly more exposition), so I rate it as well-written with minor presentation issues. | B(4): From the summary, the paper is well structured (method, experiments, conclusion) with its main contributions stated early and clear. Notation and model description appear consistent and well-defined, and figures/tables (including experiments on CoNLL 2008/2009 and predicate statistics) seem informative. Writing quality is good and hyperparameters/related work are reported adequately. Minor presentation issues remain (occasional jargon and brief treatment of some limitations), preventing a top score.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work sits squarely within a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it proposes a novel transition-based joint syntactic+semantic parser using stack LSTMs, evaluates on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2008/2009), and outperforms established baselines (Henderson et al., Lluís et al., Lewis et al.). The paper follows community standards for empirical validation (shared tasks, F1 metrics, pretrained embeddings, reported hyperparameters) and even provides code, which aligns with the reproducibility and implementation concerns I prioritize. Claims about linear-time greedy inference and concrete limitations are explicitly stated, making the contribution and audience targeting appropriate for a leading conference. | B(5): This paper is squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP/ML venue: joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing (SRL) is a core NLP problem and the work evaluates on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2008/2009) with expected metrics. The methodology (transition-based parser with stack LSTMs, end-to-end training, linear-time greedy inference) and comparisons to prior joint models (Henderson et al., Lluís et al., Lewis et al.) match the community’s methods and evaluation standards. The paper is well positioned for the audience interested in syntax–semantics integration, provides code and hyperparameters, and discusses limitations relevant to practitioners and researchers.

reviewer_a.justification: This work sits squarely within a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it proposes a novel transition-based joint syntactic+semantic parser using stack LSTMs, evaluates on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2008/2009), and outperforms established baselines (Henderson et al., Lluís et al., Lewis et al.). The paper follows community standards for empirical validation (shared tasks, F1 metrics, pretrained embeddings, reported hyperparameters) and even provides code, which aligns with the reproducibility and implementation concerns I prioritize. Claims about linear-time greedy inference and concrete limitations are explicitly stated, making the contribution and audience targeting appropriate for a leading conference.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the experimental setup is strong: evaluations on CoNLL 2008/2009 with pretrained embeddings, report of hyperparameters, and public code support the main claims about accuracy and linear-time greedy inference. However the summary does not indicate systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses of model components (e.g., contribution of stack LSTMs, action history, or embedding choices), which I would expect to fully validate architectural decisions. Related work coverage and discussion of some failure modes (transition encoding errors, predicate-sense fallback) are present, and reporting plus code make reproduction feasible, so I mark this as solid with minor gaps. | B(4): The experimental evaluation is broad (CoNLL-2008 and 2009 English shared tasks) and demonstrates improvements over prior joint models, with pretrained embeddings across languages and reported hyperparameters and released code supporting reproducibility. Related work appears adequately covered and the paper explicitly discusses concrete limitations (the 1.5% of sentences with unencodable gold transitions and the predicate-sense fallback behavior). However, the summary does not indicate any ablation or sensitivity analyses to quantify the contributions of key modeling choices (e.g., stack LSTMs, separate data structures, or greedy inference), which would be important for understanding where gains arise. Overall the evidence is strong but missing some targeted analyses, so I rate it a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the experimental setup is strong: evaluations on CoNLL 2008/2009 with pretrained embeddings, report of hyperparameters, and public code support the main claims about accuracy and linear-time greedy inference. However the summary does not indicate systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses of model components (e.g., contribution of stack LSTMs, action history, or embedding choices), which I would expect to fully validate architectural decisions. Related work coverage and discussion of some failure modes (transition encoding errors, predicate-sense fallback) are present, and reporting plus code make reproduction feasible, so I mark this as solid with minor gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is broad (CoNLL-2008 and 2009 English shared tasks) and demonstrates improvements over prior joint models, with pretrained embeddings across languages and reported hyperparameters and released code supporting reproducibility. Related work appears adequately covered and the paper explicitly discusses concrete limitations (the 1.5% of sentences with unencodable gold transitions and the predicate-sense fallback behavior). However, the summary does not indicate any ablation or sensitivity analyses to quantify the contributions of key modeling choices (e.g., stack LSTMs, separate data structures, or greedy inference), which would be important for understanding where gains arise. Overall the evidence is strong but missing some targeted analyses, so I rate it a 4.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against several strong, relevant baselines (Henderson et al. 2013, Lluís et al. 2013, Lewis et al. 2015) using the same CoNLL 2008/2009 datasets and standard SRL/parsing metrics, and reports hyperparameters and makes code available, supporting fair replication. It also openly notes limitations (transition-sequence encoding errors and predicate-sense fallback), which shows awareness when prior methods might have advantages. Missing from the summary is evidence of multiple runs/statistical tests or whether any very recent competing approaches beyond 2015 were included, so comparisons are good but not fully exhaustive. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent joint baselines (Henderson et al. 2013, Lluis et al. 2013, Lewis et al. 2015) on the standard CoNLL 2008/2009 shared tasks using the same datasets and F1 metrics, which supports fair evaluation. The manuscript explains methodological differences (transition-based stack LSTM encoding versus prior synchronized/graph-based and CCG approaches) and reports outperforming prior joint models, and it provides code and hyperparameters for reproducibility. It does not, however, explicitly discuss cases where prior methods match or exceed its performance, nor does the summary indicate deeper error analyses contrasting strengths/weaknesses across baselines, so comparisons are good but not fully exhaustive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against several strong, relevant baselines (Henderson et al. 2013, Lluís et al. 2013, Lewis et al. 2015) using the same CoNLL 2008/2009 datasets and standard SRL/parsing metrics, and reports hyperparameters and makes code available, supporting fair replication. It also openly notes limitations (transition-sequence encoding errors and predicate-sense fallback), which shows awareness when prior methods might have advantages. Missing from the summary is evidence of multiple runs/statistical tests or whether any very recent competing approaches beyond 2015 were included, so comparisons are good but not fully exhaustive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent joint baselines (Henderson et al. 2013, Lluis et al. 2013, Lewis et al. 2015) on the standard CoNLL 2008/2009 shared tasks using the same datasets and F1 metrics, which supports fair evaluation. The manuscript explains methodological differences (transition-based stack LSTM encoding versus prior synchronized/graph-based and CCG approaches) and reports outperforming prior joint models, and it provides code and hyperparameters for reproducibility. It does not, however, explicitly discuss cases where prior methods match or exceed its performance, nor does the summary indicate deeper error analyses contrasting strengths/weaknesses across baselines, so comparisons are good but not fully exhaustive.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears mostly reproducible: hyperparameters and model configurations are reported, training uses SGD/backprop and stack LSTMs, and the datasets (CoNLL-2008/2009 and Gigaword for embeddings) are publicly available; code is also provided which should resolve many implementation ambiguities. However, the summary does not confirm that the data preprocessing pipeline and all implementation-level details (e.g., exact preprocessing steps, seed usage, handling of the 1.5% of sentences with invalid transition encodings, and any training/optimization schedules) are fully specified, so a reproducer may need to rely on the released code or make minor assumptions. Evaluation metrics and procedures are standard and clearly reported (F1, predicate ID), so final evaluation should be straightforward to match. | B(5): From the provided summary, the paper supplies code, reports hyperparameters, uses publicly available CoNLL datasets and Gigaword embeddings, and precisely states evaluation metrics and procedures. Model architecture, training method (end-to-end backprop with SGD), and a predicate-sense fallback are described, enabling reimplementation if needed. The only minor quibble is the noted 1.5% of sentences whose gold joint parse cannot be encoded by the transition set (documented), but this does not impede reproducing the reported experiments given the available code and details. Overall, the work appears fully reproducible based on the summary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears mostly reproducible: hyperparameters and model configurations are reported, training uses SGD/backprop and stack LSTMs, and the datasets (CoNLL-2008/2009 and Gigaword for embeddings) are publicly available; code is also provided which should resolve many implementation ambiguities. However, the summary does not confirm that the data preprocessing pipeline and all implementation-level details (e.g., exact preprocessing steps, seed usage, handling of the 1.5% of sentences with invalid transition encodings, and any training/optimization schedules) are fully specified, so a reproducer may need to rely on the released code or make minor assumptions. Evaluation metrics and procedures are standard and clearly reported (F1, predicate ID), so final evaluation should be straightforward to match.

reviewer_b.justification: From the provided summary, the paper supplies code, reports hyperparameters, uses publicly available CoNLL datasets and Gigaword embeddings, and precisely states evaluation metrics and procedures. Model architecture, training method (end-to-end backprop with SGD), and a predicate-sense fallback are described, enabling reimplementation if needed. The only minor quibble is the noted 1.5% of sentences whose gold joint parse cannot be encoded by the transition set (documented), but this does not impede reproducing the reported experiments given the available code and details. Overall, the work appears fully reproducible based on the summary.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable novelty by combining a transition-based joint syntactic+semantic parser with stack LSTM encodings of all parser data structures to produce a finite-length state representation used for greedy, linear-time inference—this is a non-trivial architectural advance over prior synchronized transition (Henderson et al. 2013) and graph-based joint models. The formulation addresses a well-studied problem (joint SRL and syntax) but distinguishes itself clearly from earlier work by replacing hand-designed feature extraction with learned stack LSTM state representations and showing improved CoNLL 2008/2009 performance. From an implementation and methodological perspective this would meaningfully inform experts familiar with existing joint models, though it builds on established components (transition systems, LSTMs) rather than introducing an entirely new problem formulation. | B(4): The paper applies a modern neural encoding (stack LSTMs) to the longstanding problem of joint syntactic dependency parsing and semantic role labeling, yielding a transition-based, linear-time, greedy parser that outperforms prior joint models (e.g., Henderson et al. 2013, Lluís et al. 2013, Lewis et al. 2015) on CoNLL 2008/2009. This is a non-trivial methodological extension beyond earlier synchronized transition or graph-based joint approaches because it learns a single finite-length vector state representation to drive parsing decisions end-to-end. The formulation itself addresses a well-studied problem, but the integration of stack LSTMs for joint syntax+SRL and the empirical gains make the contribution a notable innovation that would interest experts in joint syntactic/semantic parsing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable novelty by combining a transition-based joint syntactic+semantic parser with stack LSTM encodings of all parser data structures to produce a finite-length state representation used for greedy, linear-time inference—this is a non-trivial architectural advance over prior synchronized transition (Henderson et al. 2013) and graph-based joint models. The formulation addresses a well-studied problem (joint SRL and syntax) but distinguishes itself clearly from earlier work by replacing hand-designed feature extraction with learned stack LSTM state representations and showing improved CoNLL 2008/2009 performance. From an implementation and methodological perspective this would meaningfully inform experts familiar with existing joint models, though it builds on established components (transition systems, LSTMs) rather than introducing an entirely new problem formulation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies a modern neural encoding (stack LSTMs) to the longstanding problem of joint syntactic dependency parsing and semantic role labeling, yielding a transition-based, linear-time, greedy parser that outperforms prior joint models (e.g., Henderson et al. 2013, Lluís et al. 2013, Lewis et al. 2015) on CoNLL 2008/2009. This is a non-trivial methodological extension beyond earlier synchronized transition or graph-based joint approaches because it learns a single finite-length vector state representation to drive parsing decisions end-to-end. The formulation itself addresses a well-studied problem, but the integration of stack LSTMs for joint syntax+SRL and the empirical gains make the contribution a notable innovation that would interest experts in joint syntactic/semantic parsing.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with empirical results on CoNLL 2008/2009 showing improvements over prior joint models, and provides code and hyperparameters which aids reproducibility. Mathematical components are straightforward (stack LSTMs) and correctly applied, and baselines are appropriate. However, I note missing statistical rigor (no mention of multiple runs or significance testing) and a small implementation limitation (1.5% of sentences cannot be encoded by the transition sequence plus a predicate-sense fallback heuristics) that are not fully analyzed. These are minor issues that do not overturn the main conclusions but weaken claims of robustness and generality. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly supported by experiments (surpassing prior joint models on CoNLL-2008/2009) and the method is technically coherent (stack LSTM encoding of parser state, end-to-end training, linear-time greedy inference). Mathematical content is procedural rather than proof-heavy and appears consistent; experimental methodology uses appropriate prior joint baselines and reports hyperparameters and code. Minor technical concerns remain: a nontrivial 1.5% of sentences cannot be encoded by the transition scheme and there is no mention of statistical significance testing for reported gains; the predicate-sense fallback policy is another pragmatic but potentially impactful approximation. These issues do not overturn the paper's main technical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with empirical results on CoNLL 2008/2009 showing improvements over prior joint models, and provides code and hyperparameters which aids reproducibility. Mathematical components are straightforward (stack LSTMs) and correctly applied, and baselines are appropriate. However, I note missing statistical rigor (no mention of multiple runs or significance testing) and a small implementation limitation (1.5% of sentences cannot be encoded by the transition sequence plus a predicate-sense fallback heuristics) that are not fully analyzed. These are minor issues that do not overturn the main conclusions but weaken claims of robustness and generality.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly supported by experiments (surpassing prior joint models on CoNLL-2008/2009) and the method is technically coherent (stack LSTM encoding of parser state, end-to-end training, linear-time greedy inference). Mathematical content is procedural rather than proof-heavy and appears consistent; experimental methodology uses appropriate prior joint baselines and reports hyperparameters and code. Minor technical concerns remain: a nontrivial 1.5% of sentences cannot be encoded by the transition scheme and there is no mention of statistical significance testing for reported gains; the predicate-sense fallback policy is another pragmatic but potentially impactful approximation. These issues do not overturn the paper's main technical claims.

main_claims: A transition-based incremental parser that jointly produces syntactic dependency trees and semantic role (SRL) graphs.
The model learns a finite-length vector representation of the entire parser state using stack LSTMs to inform transition decisions.
Greedy inference with the proposed model runs in linear time in sentence length (including feature extraction).
On the CoNLL 2008 and 2009 English shared tasks, the model surpasses previous joint models for syntax and semantics.
_summary.methodology: A transition-based, left-to-right greedy parser that encodes the four parser data structures (syntactic stack, semantic stack, buffer, and action history) with stack LSTMs; a classifier (linear scores plus biases over allowed transitions) selects transitions at each timestep and is trained end-to-end with backpropagation and SGD.
datasets: Gigaword (English AFP, German, Spanish, Chinese) - used for pretrained embeddings
baselines: Henderson et al. (2013) (synchronized transition-based joint parser)
metrics: Parsing/SRL performance on CoNLL 2008/2009 shared tasks
limitations: For 1.5% of English sentences in the CoNLL 2009 dataset, the transition sequence incorrectly encodes the gold-standard joint parse.
Predicate sense fallback for unseen predicates defaults to the “01” sense (which is correct for 91.22% of predicates by type in CoNLL 2009), potentially causing errors for the remaining cases.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing (semantic role labeling)

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a decade of experience building and evaluating structured prediction systems and deploying NLP pipelines in research and industry settings. Worked on reproducible implementations of transition-based parsers and deep learning toolkits.
traits: insists on clear, reproducible experimental details (hyperparameters, random seeds, code/data release)
looks for strong baselines, ablation studies, and error analyses to justify claims about model components
evaluates statistical rigor (multiple runs, significance or confidence intervals) and runtime/complexity claims
checks implementation-oriented claims (e.g., linear-time inference including feature extraction) and practical constraints (memory, batching)
values diagnostics that explain when and why the model succeeds or fails (qualitative examples, breakdown by sentence length/structure)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments support the claims and whether the work is reproducible and practically sound.

reviewer_b.name: Structured Semantics Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Academic researcher specializing in syntactic and semantic parsing, semantic role labeling, and the intersection of linguistics and machine learning. Familiar with prior joint and pipeline approaches for syntax and semantics and their linguistic implications.
traits: assesses novelty and positioning relative to prior joint/pipeline SRL and dependency parsing work
values conceptual clarity about what joint modeling gains (e.g., error propagation mitigation, linguistic consistency)
notices whether modeling choices respect linguistic structure and whether evaluation captures semantic adequacy (beyond labeled F1)
considers broader impact on the research agenda (does it enable new applications or settle open questions?)
looks for comparisons to the state of the art and discussion of limitations and future directions
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's novelty, theoretical contribution, and significance to the field of syntactic/semantic parsing.
